Smart Cloud Storage

Secure Your Data

A mobile cloud storage app that lets users back up, manage, and restore their files — documents, images, videos, music, and contacts — all from one clean, intuitive interface.

Role
UI/UX Designer
Timeline
2 Weeks
Platform
Mobile (iOS/Android)
Category
Productivity & Utility
Tools
Figma

Project Overview

With the average smartphone user storing thousands of photos, documents, and contacts on their device, data loss is a very real and very personal pain point. People lose data due to lost phones, accidental deletions, or device failures — and many don't realize it until it's too late.

Smart Cloud Storage was designed to make data backup feel effortless and automatic. By giving users clear visibility into both their cloud and phone storage, and making backup and restore a single tap away, the app removes the anxiety of data loss and puts users in full control of their digital lives.

The Problem

Through user research and competitive analysis of apps like Google One, iCloud, and Dropbox, I identified key frustrations users face with existing cloud storage solutions:

  • No Clear Storage Overview: Users rarely know how much storage they've used or how it's distributed across file types
  • Confusing Backup Flows: Triggering a backup or restore is buried in multiple menus, causing users to skip it entirely
  • Lack of Dual View: Most apps show only cloud storage — users have no visibility into their phone storage alongside it
  • Opaque Pricing: Upgrade prompts appear without clearly explaining what each tier offers or how much storage users actually need

Target Audience

Primary Users: Everyday smartphone users (ages 18–50) who want peace of mind that their photos, contacts, and important files are safely backed up — without needing to understand the technical details.

Secondary Users: Professionals and small business users who manage documents and media files across devices and need reliable, accessible cloud storage with easy restore capabilities.

Design Process

1. Research & Discovery

I began by auditing the top cloud storage apps on the market — analyzing their onboarding flows, home screen layouts, and paywall designs. The consistent finding was that these apps prioritize features over clarity, leaving users uncertain about their storage status and how to act on it.

The design opportunity was clear: build an app where the most important information — how much storage you have, what's using it, and what to do about it — is visible at a glance.

2. User Flows

I mapped the core journeys before touching any visual design:

  • Splash → Onboarding (3 steps) → Home Dashboard
  • Home (Cloud View) ↔ Home (Phone Storage View)
  • Home → Backup Your Data / Restore Your Data
  • Profile → Go Premium → Paywall → Subscribe
  • Settings → Privacy Policy / Feedback / Language

3. Splash Screen

The splash screen is minimal and purposeful — a cloud icon with upload/download arrows immediately communicates the app's core function: moving data between device and cloud. The tagline "Secure your data" reinforces the emotional promise of the app before the user even reaches onboarding.

4. Onboarding Flow

The three-screen onboarding introduces the app's core value propositions progressively — Free Storage, Backup & Restore, and Secure Storage. Each screen pairs a bold icon with a focused headline and a one-sentence supporting description, keeping cognitive load minimal. The asymmetric Next button placement and dot pagination guide users through without friction.

Free Storage
Backup & Restore
Secure Storage

Key Features & Screens

Home Screen — Cloud Storage & Phone Storage

The home screen is the heart of the app. A donut chart at the top provides an immediate, visual breakdown of total vs. used vs. available storage — making the user's storage situation legible in under a second. Below it, file category cards (Documents, Images, Videos, Music, Contacts) give a precise breakdown of what's consuming space.

A key design decision was the dual-tab navigation at the bottom — switching between Cloud Storage and Phone Storage views. This allows users to compare their device storage against their cloud backup, helping them understand what's been backed up and what still needs attention. The primary CTA — "Backup Your Data" or "Restore Your Data" — changes contextually depending on which view the user is in.

Cloud Storage View
Phone Storage View

Settings & Profile Screen

The settings screen combines user profile information with quick-access settings in a clean list layout. A prominent "Go Premium" banner at the top of settings acts as a persistent but non-intrusive upgrade prompt — reinforcing the value of premium (Unlimited Cloud Storage) every time the user visits their profile. Settings options are clearly labeled with colored icons for fast scanning.

Paywall — Go Premium

The paywall offers three storage tiers — Standard (100 GB), Professional (500 GB), and Family (1000 GB) — with a Monthly/Yearly toggle allowing users to choose their billing preference. Benefits are listed clearly upfront, and trust signals (Cancel Anytime, No Hidden Charges, Secure Payments) address the most common objections right at the point of decision. The selected plan is highlighted with a blue border to provide clear visual feedback.

Design Decisions

Color Palette

The color system was designed to feel trustworthy, modern, and clean — essential for an app handling personal data:

#2563EB
Primary Blue — Brand & CTAs
#60A5FA
Sky Blue — Accents & Charts
#EDE9FE
Lavender — Card Backgrounds
#FFFFFF
White — Base Backgrounds

Why this palette? Blue anchors the app in trust and reliability — the same visual language used by leading cloud and security brands. The soft lavender card backgrounds add warmth and distinction to the file category grid without competing with the blue primary actions. The overall light theme keeps the interface feeling open and spacious.

The Donut Chart

Rather than showing storage as a number alone, the donut chart transforms an abstract metric into something immediately understandable — users can see at a glance how full their storage is before reading a single number. This visual-first approach reduces the cognitive effort required to understand one's storage situation.

Dual Storage Views

Showing cloud and phone storage side-by-side (via bottom tab navigation) was a deliberate decision to give users the full picture. Most competing apps show only cloud storage, leaving users unaware of their device's local situation. By surfacing both, the app naturally creates urgency to back up when the phone storage is nearly full — a core driver of engagement and premium conversion.

Challenges & Solutions

Challenge 1

Problem: Making storage data — an inherently abstract concept — immediately understandable at a glance

Solution 1

Used a donut chart as the hero element of the home screen, making used vs. available storage visually obvious before the user reads any numbers

Challenge 2

Problem: Designing a paywall that feels informative rather than aggressive, without undermining the free experience

Solution 2

Placed the premium prompt inside the Settings/Profile screen as a card — visible but non-intrusive — rather than as a full-screen interstitial, respecting the user's current task

Challenge 3

Problem: Surfacing the right primary action (Backup vs. Restore) without overwhelming users with choices

Solution 3

Made the primary CTA button contextual — "Backup Your Data" on the Phone Storage view and "Restore Your Data" on the Cloud Storage view — so the right action is always prominent depending on what the user is looking at

Challenge 4

Problem: Communicating three different onboarding messages without making the flow feel long or tedious

Solution 4

Each onboarding screen focuses on exactly one benefit with a single icon, one bold headline, and one sentence of supporting text — making each screen scannable in under 3 seconds

Design Principles

Clarity Over Cleverness

Every screen surfaces the most important information first. Storage status, file categories, and primary actions are never hidden behind menus

Contextual Actions

The app's primary CTA changes based on what the user is viewing — Backup when on phone storage, Restore when on cloud — reducing decision fatigue

Trust Through Transparency

Storage numbers, pricing, and plan details are all shown clearly and upfront — no hidden caps, no surprise charges, no vague promises

Calm, Non-Pushy Monetization

The premium upgrade path is always accessible but never intrusive — placed in profile settings rather than blocking the core experience

Key Takeaways

  • Visualize abstract data: Storage numbers become meaningful when paired with a chart — the donut visualization transforms a stat into a feeling
  • Dual perspective builds urgency naturally: Showing both cloud and phone storage side by side creates a natural motivation to back up without any pushy messaging
  • Context-sensitive CTAs reduce friction: A single button that adapts to the user's current view is far more effective than presenting multiple backup/restore options at once
  • Respectful paywalls convert better: Embedding the upgrade prompt in the profile rather than as a pop-up builds trust and leads to more intentional upgrade decisions

Impact & Results

This project demonstrates my ability to design a complete, multi-screen productivity app where data clarity and user confidence are the central design challenges.

  • Designed a complete end-to-end experience from splash screen to premium subscription
  • Created a dual storage view system that gives users unprecedented visibility into their data
  • Built a transparent, tiered paywall with storage-based plan differentiation (100 GB / 500 GB / 1000 GB)
  • Established a consistent blue and lavender design system across all 9 screens
  • Designed contextual primary actions that adapt based on user context, reducing decision fatigue

Next Steps

If this project were to move forward, I would:

  • Design the backup in-progress screen with real-time progress indicators and estimated time remaining
  • Build out individual file category screens — tapping Documents, Images, Videos, etc. should open a browsable file list
  • Add a file search screen allowing users to find specific files across their cloud storage quickly
  • Conduct usability testing on the donut chart to verify users correctly interpret used vs. available at a glance
  • Explore auto-backup scheduling — a settings screen where users can configure when and how often backups run automatically

Thanks for reading!
